query: how do I purify water in the wilderness
ground_truth: There are several methods to purify water in the wilderness. Boiling is the safest method: boil water for 1 minute at sea level (3 minutes above 6,500 feet elevation). You can also use water purification tablets following the provided directions, or add 5 drops of 2% tincture of iodine to a canteen of clear water (10 drops if cloudy or cold) and let it stand for 30 minutes. Household liquid bleach (5-9% sodium hypochlorite) can be used by adding 8 drops per gallon of clear water (16 drops for cloudy water), stirring and letting stand for 30 minutes. Most portable water filters can remove parasites but not bacteria or viruses, so filtered water may need additional treatment. Rainwater collected in clean containers or from plants is usually safe. Drinking unpurified water risks dysentery, cholera, typhoid, flukes, and leeches. Water contaminated with harmful chemicals or toxins cannot be made safe by boiling, disinfecting, or filtering.

query: how do I stop someone from bleeding really bad
ground_truth: To control severe bleeding, act immediately as the loss of 1 liter of blood produces moderate shock and 3 liters is usually fatal. First, expose the wound by tearing clothing away and immediately apply firm, direct pressure using gauze, clean cloth, or whatever is available. Maintain pressure long enough to seal the damaged surface; if bleeding continues after 30 minutes, apply a pressure dressing (thick gauze held in place with a tightly wrapped bandage). Do not remove the dressing even if blood-soaked. Elevate the injured extremity above heart level to slow blood loss while maintaining direct pressure. If direct pressure fails, apply pressure at a pressure point where the main artery lies near the skin surface between the wound and the heart. If all other methods fail and bleeding is life-threatening, apply a tourniquet at least 2-3 inches above the wound (between the wound and the heart), never over a joint. Tighten only enough to stop blood flow. Use a windlass rod to increase pressure, secure it, and note the time of application. Do not remove the tourniquet; seek medical aid immediately.

query: what should I do if bitten by a snake
ground_truth: If bitten by a snake, seek emergency medical attention as soon as possible to start antivenom treatment. Keep calm, as excitement and panic speed up circulation and cause the body to absorb toxin more quickly. Lay or sit down with the bite in a neutral, comfortable position. Remove watches, rings, bracelets, or other constricting items before swelling starts. Wash the bite with soap and water and cover it with a clean, dry dressing. Mark the leading edge of tenderness/swelling on the skin and write the time alongside it. If you determine a poisonous snake caused the bite, set up for shock and force fluids, maintain an airway (especially if bitten near the face or neck), use a constricting band between the wound and the heart, and immobilize the site. Do NOT pick up the snake or try to trap it, do NOT apply a tourniquet or ice, do NOT cut the wound or attempt to suck out the venom, and do NOT drink alcohol as a pain killer. Signs of a poisonous bite include puncture marks from fangs, spontaneous bleeding, pain and swelling at the site, and later breathing difficulty, paralysis, weakness, or numbness.

query: how to build a shelter in the woods
ground_truth: When building a shelter in the woods, start looking for a site as soon as you realize shelter is a priority. The site must contain materials to make the shelter and be large enough and level enough for you to lie down comfortably. Use the BLISS acronym for site selection: Blend in with surroundings, Low silhouette, Irregular shape, Small, and Secluded location. Avoid flash flood areas, avalanche zones, and sites below the high water mark. Consider natural formations first, such as caves, rocky crevices, large trees with low-hanging limbs, or fallen trees with thick branches, but check for poisonous snakes, loose rocks, and dead limbs. For a field-expedient lean-to, use available natural materials without needing manufactured items. A poncho lean-to requires only a poncho, rope, and two trees and takes minimal time to build. A debris hut is one of the best shelters for warmth and ease of construction. When considering the type of shelter, weigh how much time and effort is needed, whether it will protect from the elements (sun, wind, rain, snow), and whether you have the necessary tools and materials.

query: how to start a fire without matches
ground_truth: There are several primitive methods to start a fire without matches. The flint and steel method is the most reliable direct spark method: strike a flint or other hard, sharp-edged rock with a piece of carbon steel (stainless steel will not produce a good spark) using a loose-jointed wrist. When a spark catches in the tinder, blow on it to spread the spark into flames. The fire-plow is a friction method: cut a straight groove in a softwood base and plow the blunt tip of a hardwood shaft up and down the groove, applying pressure until friction ignites the wood particles. A convex lens (from binoculars, camera, telescopic sights, or magnifying glass) can be used on bright sunny days by angling the lens to concentrate sun rays on tinder until it smolders. A metal match can produce sparks when scraped with a knife onto dry tinder. A battery can generate sparks by touching bare wire ends from each terminal together near tinder. You need three types of fire material: tinder (absolutely dry material that ignites with a spark), kindling (readily combustible material to increase temperature), and fuel (less combustible material that burns slowly and steadily).

query: what food is safe to eat in the wild
ground_truth: Plants provide carbohydrates and are the main source of energy in survival situations. Many plant foods such as nuts and seeds provide enough protein and oils for normal efficiency. Before eating unknown plants, use the Universal Edibility Test, which requires more than 24 hours per plant part to complete. To avoid poisonous plants, stay away from any wild or unknown plants with milky or discolored sap, beans/bulbs/seeds inside pods, bitter or soapy taste, spines/fine hairs/thorns, dill/carrot/parsnip/parsleylike foliage, 'almond' scent in woody parts, grain heads with pink/purplish/black spurs, or three-leaved growth patterns. Common edible wild plants include dandelion, cattail, blackberries, blueberries, wild onion and garlic, chestnuts, oaks (acorns), asparagus, and plantain. All species of birds are edible, and fish represent a good source of protein and fat. Meat is more nourishing than plant food. Learn as much as possible about the plant life of areas where you expect to be traveling or working before you go.

query: what do I put in an emergency kit
ground_truth: A basic emergency supplies kit should include: water (one gallon per person per day for at least three days), food (at least a three-day supply of non-perishable food), battery-powered or hand crank radio and a NOAA Weather Radio with tone alert, flashlight, first aid kit, extra batteries, whistle (to signal for help), dust mask (to filter contaminated air), plastic sheeting and duct tape (to shelter in place), moist towelettes/garbage bags/plastic ties (for personal sanitation), manual can opener, local maps, and cell phone with chargers and backup battery. Additional items include: a portable battery-powered radio or television, sanitation and hygiene items, matches in a waterproof container, extra clothing, kitchen accessories and cooking utensils, photocopies of identification and credit cards, cash and coins, special needs items such as prescription medications and eyeglasses, items for infants, and sleeping bags or warm blankets. Store items in airtight plastic bags and put your entire kit in one or two easy-to-carry containers such as plastic bins or a duffel bag.

query: how to signal for help when lost
ground_truth: When lost or in a survival situation, use both visual and audio signals. During darkness, fire is the most effective visual signal: build three fires in a triangle or straight line (the international distress signal) with about 25 meters between fires. During daylight, use smoke to gain attention; three columns of smoke is the international distress signal. Create smoke that contrasts with the background: smother a fire with green leaves, moss, or water for white smoke, or add rubber or oil-soaked rags for black smoke. A signal mirror is one of the most effective daytime signaling devices and can be seen for miles. Man-made geometric patterns (straight lines, circles, triangles, or X's) displayed in uninhabited areas attract attention. Pen flares fire about 150 meters high and sound like a pistol shot. Red star clusters are preferred as red is the international distress color. If available, use tracer ammunition or smoke grenades. For audio signals, use a whistle, which carries further than voice. Always have visual and audio signals ready for use, and pick locations visible from the air on the highest possible terrain.

query: how do I find north without a compass
ground_truth: You can find north without a compass using the sun and shadows. The shadow-tip method is simple and accurate: place a 1-meter stick in level ground where it casts a shadow. Mark the shadow's tip (this first mark is always west). Wait 10-15 minutes, then mark the new shadow tip position. Draw a straight line through the two marks for an approximate east-west line. Stand with the first mark (west) to your left and the second mark to your right, and you are now facing north. This works everywhere on earth. An alternate, more accurate shadow method requires more time: mark the morning shadow tip, draw an arc through it around the stick, then in the afternoon mark where the shadow touches the arc again. A line through these two marks gives an accurate east-west line. In the northern hemisphere, the sun is due south at its highest point in the sky (noon), and shadows move clockwise. In the southern hemisphere, the noonday sun marks due north, and shadows move counterclockwise. At night, you can use the North Star (Polaris) in the northern hemisphere. The earth's relationship to the sun provides the basis for all these field-expedient direction finding methods.

query: how to treat a burn
ground_truth: Burns are classified by type (thermal, electrical, chemical, laser) and severity (superficial, partial thickness, full thickness). First, eliminate the source of the burn. For thermal burns, remove the casualty from the fire, smother flames by rolling the casualty on the ground, and use nonsynthetic material to cover them (synthetic materials like nylon may melt and cause further injury). For electrical burns, turn off the electrical current before touching the casualty. Cool the burn by immersing in cool water for not more than one minute or covering with clean compresses soaked in cool water (do not use ice as it causes vessel constriction). Avoid rapid temperature changes that can cause shock. Dress the burn by covering loosely with dry, sterile dressings to keep air out, reduce pain, and prevent infection. Wrap fingers and toes loosely and individually for severe hand/foot burns. Do not apply antiseptics, ointments, or other remedies. Do not remove shreds of tissue, break blisters, or remove adhered particles of clothing. Loosen clothing near the affected area and remove jewelry. The objectives of burn first aid are to prevent hypothermia, manage pain, and reduce risk of infection.

query: what to do during a tornado
ground_truth: During a tornado warning, take immediate action. Move to an interior room on the lowest floor of a sturdy building and avoid windows. At home, go to your basement, safe room, or an interior room away from windows. At work or school, follow your tornado drill and proceed to the tornado shelter quickly and calmly; stay away from windows and do not go to large open rooms such as cafeterias, gymnasiums, or auditoriums. If outdoors, seek shelter inside a sturdy building immediately; sheds and storage facilities are NOT safe. If in a vehicle, the best course of action is to drive to the closest shelter. If you cannot reach safe shelter, either get down in your car and cover your head, or abandon your car and seek shelter in a low-lying area such as a ditch or ravine. Before a tornado, create a family plan that includes an emergency meeting place and shelter spaces, and practice tornado drills regularly. Continue to listen to local news or a NOAA Weather Radio for updates. After a tornado, stay informed about additional tornado watches, assess damage wearing protective clothing and sturdy shoes, stay out of damaged buildings, and watch for dangerous debris like sharp metal, glass, or downed power lines.

query: how to keep food safe during a power outage
ground_truth: During a power outage, keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed as much as possible. If doors stay closed, food will stay safe in the refrigerator for up to 4 hours and in a full freezer for approximately 48 hours (24 hours if half full). Keep appliance thermometers in your refrigerator (at 40 degrees F or below) and freezer (at 0 degrees F or below). After the power returns, never taste food to determine if it is safe; when in doubt, throw it out. Throw away perishable food that has not been refrigerated or frozen properly, food that has an unusual odor/color/texture, and food that has been at room temperature for more than 2 hours. Throw away any food that may have come in contact with flood or storm water. Discard items that include meat, poultry, fish, eggs, and leftovers if they have been above 40 degrees F for 2 hours or more. If the power will be out for an extended period, consider using coolers with ice; 25 pounds of dry ice will keep a 10-cubic-foot freezer below freezing for 3-4 days. Group food together in the freezer to help it stay cold longer.

query: what to do in a flood
ground_truth: Before a flood, avoid building in a floodplain unless you elevate and reinforce your home. Elevate the furnace, water heater, and electric panel if susceptible to flooding. Install check valves in sewer traps to prevent flood water from backing up. Seal basement walls with waterproofing compounds. If a flood is likely, listen to radio or television for information. Be aware that flash flooding can occur; if there is any possibility of a flash flood, move immediately to higher ground without waiting for instructions. If you must prepare to evacuate, secure your home by moving essential items to the upper floor and turning off utilities at the main switches if instructed to do so. Do not walk through moving water; six inches of moving water can knock you down. Do not drive into flooded areas; six inches of water reaches the bottom of most cars, a foot of water will float many vehicles, and two feet of rushing water can carry away most vehicles including SUVs. After a flood, avoid floodwaters as they may be contaminated by oil, gasoline, or raw sewage, and may be electrically charged from downed power lines. Return home only when authorities say it is safe, and stay out of any buildings surrounded by floodwater.

query: how to give CPR to someone
ground_truth: To perform rescue breathing (a key component of CPR), first establish unresponsiveness and call for help. Position the casualty on their back and open the airway using the jaw-thrust technique (grasping the angles of the lower jaw and lifting with both hands) or the head-tilt/chin-lift technique (tilting the head back and lifting the chin). Check for breathing by looking for chest rise and fall, listening for air escaping, and feeling for airflow on your cheek for 3-5 seconds. If the casualty is not breathing, pinch the nostrils together, maintain the backward head tilt, take a deep breath, and place your mouth in an airtight seal around the casualty's mouth. Blow two full breaths (1 to 1.5 seconds per breath), watching for the chest to rise. If the casualty has a severe jaw fracture or mouth wound, use the mouth-to-nose method instead. Check for a pulse; if the heart has stopped (no pulse, unconscious, limp, pupils wide open), seek medical help immediately as seconds count. If a neck injury is suspected, use the jaw-thrust technique to avoid further spinal damage. Continue rescue breathing and monitor the casualty until medical help arrives.

query: how to stay safe in extreme heat
ground_truth: During extreme heat (temperatures hovering 10 degrees or more above average high), take several precautions. Drink plenty of fluids regardless of activity level; do not wait until you feel thirsty. Avoid very cold drinks as they can cause stomach cramps. Replace salt and minerals lost through heavy sweating (sports drinks can help, but consult a doctor if on a low-salt diet). Wear lightweight, light-colored, loose-fitting clothing and sunscreen (SPF 15 or higher). Schedule outdoor activities for morning and evening hours, and rest in shady areas. Pace yourself if not accustomed to working in heat; stop activity if you feel faint or weak. Stay indoors in air-conditioning if possible; if your home lacks AC, go to a public cooling center. Use a buddy system to monitor each other. Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency where the body temperature rises rapidly above 103 degrees F; symptoms include hot/red/dry skin and unconsciousness. Call 911 immediately and cool the victim rapidly. Heat exhaustion symptoms include heavy sweating, paleness, weakness, nausea, and dizziness; move the person to a cool area, apply cool compresses, and give cool nonalcoholic beverages. Depending on heat and activity, you may need 0.5 to 1.25 quarts of water per hour.

query: tourniquet application steps
ground_truth: Apply a tourniquet only when direct pressure and all other bleeding control methods have failed, or when a limb has been amputated. Step 1: Expose the wound and immediately apply firm, direct pressure to try to stop the bleeding. Step 2: If bleeding does not stop, place a tourniquet at least 2-3 inches above the wound, between the wound and the heart. The tourniquet may be applied over clothing. Step 3: Adjust the tourniquet position, ensuring it is not placed over a joint (elbow, knee, wrist, or ankle) since joints protect blood passageways. For maximum effectiveness, place on the upper arm or above the knee on the thigh. Step 4: Manually tighten the tourniquet using the buckle and pull firmly on the end strap. Step 5: Use the windlass rod to further tighten by twisting in one direction to increase pressure and stop bleeding. Step 6: Secure the windlass rod using the windlass clip and velcro strap to prevent unwinding. Step 7: Note the time of application, as leaving a tourniquet on too long can cause tissue damage. Mark the casualty's forehead with a 'T' and the time. Do NOT remove or loosen the tourniquet; leave it in full view and seek medical aid immediately.

query: hypothermia treatment protocol
ground_truth: Hypothermia occurs when core body temperature falls below 95 degrees F (35 degrees C) and is classified as mild (90-95 degrees F), moderate (80-89 degrees F), or severe (below 80 degrees F). All levels are potentially life-threatening medical emergencies requiring immediate care. For mild hypothermia, get the person into a warm room or shelter, remove wet clothing, and warm the center of the body (chest, neck, head, and groin) using an electric blanket, skin-to-skin contact under loose dry layers, or warm (not alcoholic) drinks. Exchange wet clothing for dry insulated clothing, conduct physical exercise, hydrate with warm liquids, and add heat through shelter, sleeping bags, or blankets. For moderate and severe hypothermia, handle the person gently and get emergency assistance immediately. Symptoms include dilated pupils, lack of shivering, low blood pressure, unconsciousness, and abnormal heart rhythms. Do not allow the patient to exercise or assist in evacuation due to high risk of heart failure. If the person appears to have no pulse or breathing, perform CPR even if they appear dead, as hypothermia victims with core temperatures as low as 60 degrees F have been successfully resuscitated. The medical adage is 'A person is not dead until they are warm and dead.' Keep warming the person while performing CPR until medical aid becomes available.

query: wound irrigation and debridement
ground_truth: For emergency wound care, first wash your hands thoroughly with soap and clean water. Avoid touching the wound with your fingers (use disposable gloves if possible). Remove obstructive jewelry and clothing from the injured body part. Apply direct pressure to any bleeding wound to control bleeding. After bleeding has stopped, clean the wound: examine for dirt and foreign objects, gently flood the wound with bottled water or clean running water (saline solution is preferred if available), gently clean around the wound with soap and clean water, then pat dry and apply an adhesive bandage or dry clean cloth. Leave unclean wounds, bites, and punctures open, as wounds not cleaned correctly can trap bacteria and cause infection. Seek medical attention if there is a foreign object embedded in the wound, if the wound is from an animal bite or puncture by a dirty object, if signs of infection appear (pain, swelling, redness, draining, or fever), or if you have signs of sepsis. Review the wound every 24 hours. Have a healthcare professional evaluate for tetanus immunization if the wound is contaminated with feces, soil, or saliva.

query: frostbite classification and treatment
ground_truth: Frostbite is frozen body tissue that occurs when ambient air temperature is below 32 degrees F (0 degrees C). It generally occurs to exposed skin or extremities such as the nose, ears, cheeks, hands, and feet. There are four degrees of frostbite, but for field diagnosis it is classified as superficial or deep. The first sign is numbness, preceded by tingling, burning, aching, and sharp pain. In superficial frostbite, the affected area feels 'wooden,' skin initially turns red then becomes pale gray or waxy white. In deep frostbite, the tissue is hard, insensitive, and may appear white or grayish-yellow. Treatment: Professionals in a medical facility must evaluate all frostbite cases. For superficial frostbite, re-warm at room temperature or with skin-to-skin contact (covering cheeks with warm hands, placing fingers under armpits, placing bare feet against a companion's belly). For deep frostbite, move the patient to a warm shelter. If re-warming, prepare a warm water bath between 98-104 degrees F (37-40 degrees C) in a container large enough that tissue does not touch sides or bottom. Never use hot water, engine exhaust, open flames, heat packs, or steam. Avoid rubbing, massaging, or bending affected areas, and do NOT rub with snow or ice. If tissue may thaw and re-freeze (during evacuation), keep the injury frozen; re-freezing causes additional damage. Leave blisters (blebs) intact; if ruptured, treat with topical antibiotic and sterile bandage. Casualties must avoid tobacco products as they reduce blood flow to extremities.

query: hemorrhage control techniques
ground_truth: In a survival situation, control serious bleeding immediately as the victim can die within minutes. External bleeding is classified as arterial (bright red, spurting blood from arteries, most dangerous), venous (steady flow of dark red blood from veins, easier to control), and capillary (minor cuts and scrapes, not difficult to control). The primary techniques are: (1) Direct Pressure - the most effective method. Apply firm pressure directly over the wound long enough to seal the damaged surface. If bleeding continues after 30 minutes, apply a pressure dressing (thick gauze held with a tightly wrapped bandage). Do not remove it even when blood-soaked. (2) Elevation - raise the injured extremity above heart level to slow blood loss while maintaining direct pressure. (3) Pressure Points - apply digital pressure where the main artery lies near the skin surface between the wound and heart; less effective than direct pressure but useful temporarily. (4) Tourniquet - use only as a last resort when all other methods fail or for amputations. Place 2-3 inches above the wound between wound and heart, never over a joint. Tighten only enough to stop blood flow. If using an improvised tourniquet, use material at least 2 inches wide with a rigid stick to tighten. Mark the time of application. Do not loosen or release an applied tourniquet. Treat for shock and seek medical aid.

query: open pneumothorax management
ground_truth: An open pneumothorax (sucking chest wound) occurs when the chest wall is punctured, causing air to leak into the chest cavity and collapse the lung. The casualty may have pain in the chest or shoulder, difficulty breathing, coughing up blood, and a rapid or weak heartbeat; a sucking sound may be heard when breathing. Treatment requires immediately applying an occlusive dressing: place the inside surface of a plastic wrapper directly over the wound when the casualty exhales, then cover with the white side of a field dressing. Secure the dressing by taping or tying on three sides only, leaving the fourth side as a flutter valve to allow trapped air to escape. If the casualty's condition worsens after placing the dressing (difficulty breathing, restlessness, bluish/gray skin indicating tension pneumothorax), quickly lift or remove and replace the occlusive dressing. Position the casualty on the injured side or sitting up, whichever makes breathing easier, and seek medical assistance immediately.

query: heat stroke versus heat exhaustion differential
ground_truth: Heat stroke and heat exhaustion are distinct heat-related illnesses. Heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency in which the body is unable to regulate its temperature: the sweating mechanism fails, body temperature may rise to 106 degrees F or higher within 10 to 15 minutes, and death or permanent disability can result without emergency treatment. Do not give the victim fluids; instead cool rapidly by immersion, cool shower, or fanning with wet sheets, and get medical assistance immediately. Heat exhaustion is a milder form that develops after several days of high temperature exposure with inadequate fluid replacement. It is the body's response to excessive loss of water and salt through sweat. Warning signs include heavy sweating, paleness, muscle cramps, tiredness, weakness, dizziness, headache, nausea, vomiting, and fainting. The skin may be cool and moist with a fast, weak pulse and rapid, shallow breathing. Treatment includes rest in a cool environment, cool nonalcoholic beverages, cool shower or bath, and lightweight clothing. If untreated, heat exhaustion may progress to heat stroke.

query: water disinfection methods field conditions
ground_truth: Several methods can disinfect water in field conditions. Boiling is the most effective method, killing bacteria, viruses, and parasites; boil water for 1 minute at sea level (3 minutes above 6,500 feet elevation, or 10 minutes as a general rule per FM 21-76). Chemical disinfection with unscented household liquid bleach (5-9% concentration) uses 8 drops per gallon of clear water or 16 drops for cloudy water; let stand for 30 minutes before drinking. Other chemical methods include: granular calcium hypochlorite (HTH) -- dissolve one heaping teaspoon in two gallons of water to create a chlorine solution, then add 1 pint of that solution to 12.5 gallons of water (handle with care as HTH is a powerful oxidant); common household iodine (2% tincture) at 5 drops per quart for clear water or 10 drops for cloudy water, standing 30 minutes; and commercial water disinfection tablets containing chlorine, iodine, or chlorine dioxide per label instructions. Water purification tablets with iodine can also be used per the provided directions. Most portable water filters can remove parasites but not bacteria or viruses, so filtered water may need additional treatment. Water contaminated with harmful chemicals or toxins cannot be made safe by any of these methods.

query: carbon monoxide poisoning clinical presentation
ground_truth: Carbon monoxide (CO) is an odorless, colorless gas that kills without warning. The most common symptoms of CO poisoning are headache, dizziness, weakness, upset stomach, vomiting, chest pain, and confusion. CO symptoms are often described as 'flu-like.' If a person breathes in a lot of CO, it can cause them to pass out or die. People who are sleeping, drunk, or under the influence of other substances can die from CO poisoning before they develop symptoms. Infants, the elderly, and people with chronic heart disease, anemia, or breathing problems are at higher risk. CO is found in fumes produced when burning fuel in cars, trucks, small engines, stoves, lanterns, grills, fireplaces, gas ranges, or furnaces.

query: fracture immobilization and splinting
ground_truth: A fracture is any break in the continuity of a bone, which can be open (bone protrudes through skin) or closed (no open wound). Signs and symptoms include pain, tenderness, swelling, deformity, discoloration, loss of function, and grating sounds when broken ends rub together. Do not encourage the casualty to move the injured part to identify a fracture; if unsure, treat as a fracture. The primary purpose of immobilization is to prevent sharp bone edges from cutting tissue, muscle, blood vessels, and nerves, which reduces pain and helps prevent shock. The basic splinting principle is to immobilize the joints above and below the fracture. Splints may be improvised from boards, poles, sticks, tree limbs, or cardboard; padding from jackets, blankets, or leafy vegetation; and bandages from belts, rifle slings, or strips of clothing. Use at least four ties (two above and two below the fracture) secured with square knots tied away from the body. Pad splints where they touch bony parts. Check circulation below the injury before and after splinting by noting skin color, temperature, and feeling. For a thighbone fracture, an improvised traction splint can be made using forked branches to maintain alignment during healing.

query: tetanus prophylaxis wound management
ground_truth: The best ways to prevent tetanus are vaccination and proper wound management. Healthcare providers should assess the wound type, observe for signs of infection, and properly treat it by cleaning all wounds, removing dirt or foreign material, and removing or debriding necrotic material. Wounds are categorized by tetanus exposure risk: clean and minor wounds do not pose a major risk, while dirty or major wounds (contaminated with dirt, feces, soil, or saliva, or caused by punctures, avulsions, or burns) carry higher risk. No tetanus vaccination is needed if the patient received their last tetanus vaccine less than 5 years ago (for dirty wounds) or less than 10 years ago (for clean wounds) and has a complete primary series. For those with unknown, incomplete, or no vaccination history, vaccination is recommended for all wound types. Tetanus Immune Globulin (TIG) at 250 international units intramuscularly is indicated for dirty or major wounds in people with unknown, incomplete, or no vaccination history; TIG is never indicated for clean and minor wounds. Antibiotic prophylaxis against tetanus is not recommended.

query: abdominal evisceration field treatment
ground_truth: For abdominal wounds with evisceration (protruding organs), first evaluate the casualty and check for both entry and exit wounds, treating the more serious wound first. Position the casualty on his back with knees flexed (upright position) to relieve pain, assist in shock treatment, prevent further bowel exposure, and relax abdominal muscles. Expose the wound by removing loose clothing, but do not remove clothing stuck to the wound. Gently pick up any organs on the ground using a clean, dry dressing or the cleanest available material and place them on top of the casualty's abdomen. Critical warnings: do NOT probe, clean, or try to remove any foreign object from the abdomen; do NOT touch exposed organs with bare hands; do NOT push organs back inside the body. Apply the field dressing (use the casualty's, not your own) with the white sterile side directly over the wound. When tying the dressing, do NOT put pressure on the wound or exposed internal parts as this could cause vomiting or ruptured intestines; tie the tails loosely at the casualty's side, not directly over the dressing. Do NOT give casualties with abdominal wounds food or water (moistening lips is allowed). Seek medical assistance immediately.

query: how to purrify water
ground_truth: To purify water, the most effective method is boiling: bring water to a rolling boil for 1 minute (3 minutes above 6,500 feet elevation). For chemical disinfection, add 8 drops of unscented 5-9% household bleach per gallon of clear water (16 drops if cloudy); let stand 30 minutes, and if it does not smell of bleach, add 6 more drops and wait 15 more minutes. You can also use 5 drops of 2% tincture of iodine per quart of clear water (10 drops if cloudy), standing 30 minutes. Water purification tablets (chlorine, iodine, or chlorine dioxide) may be used per label instructions. Granular calcium hypochlorite (HTH) can be dissolved to create a chlorine solution. Most portable water filters remove parasites but not bacteria or viruses, so filtered water may need additional disinfection. If water is cloudy, let it settle first, then filter through a clean cloth, paper towel, or coffee filter before disinfecting. Water contaminated with harmful chemicals or toxins cannot be made safe by boiling, disinfecting, or filtering.

query: treet a broken bone
ground_truth: To treat a broken bone (fracture), first identify the type: open fractures have bone protruding through the skin (treat the wound after setting), while closed fractures have no open wound. Signs include pain, tenderness, swelling, deformity, discoloration, loss of function, and grating sounds. Immobilize the fracture by splinting above and below the break. Splints can be improvised from boards, poles, sticks, tree limbs, or cardboard. Pad splints with available material such as jackets, blankets, or leafy vegetation. Use at least four ties secured with square knots. Check circulation below the fracture before and after splinting by noting skin color, temperature, and numbness. For open fractures, treat the open wound in addition to splinting. Maintain traction on larger bones; for a broken thighbone, an improvised traction splint can be constructed from forked branches. If unsure whether a bone is fractured, treat it as a fracture. Do not encourage the casualty to move the injured part.

query: hypotermia symptoms
ground_truth: Hypothermia occurs when the body's core temperature falls below 95 degrees F (35 degrees C). Warning signs in adults include shivering, exhaustion or feeling very tired, confusion, fumbling hands, memory loss, slurred speech, and drowsiness. In babies, signs include bright red, cold skin and very low energy. Hypothermia is classified as mild (90-95 degrees F), moderate (80-89 degrees F), or severe (below 80 degrees F). Mild hypothermia symptoms include confusion, sleepiness, slurred speech, shallow breathing, weak pulse, behavior changes, intense shivering, stiffness in arms or legs, poor body movement control, slow reactions, and abnormal heart rhythms. Moderate and severe hypothermia symptoms include dilated pupils, lack of shivering, low blood pressure, unconsciousness, and absent or abnormal heart rhythms. query: what to do about a mountain lion encounter

To stay safe: do not hike or jog alone -- go in groups with adults supervising children. Keep children close and never let them run ahead, as mountain lions seem especially drawn to children. If you encounter a mountain lion, the goals are to convince it you are not prey and that you may be dangerous. Do not approach a lion -- give it a way to escape. Do not run, as running may stimulate the lion's instinct to chase. Instead, stand and face the animal and make eye contact. If you have small children, pick them up without bending over or turning away from the lion. Do not crouch down or bend over, as a person squatting resembles a four-legged prey animal. Do all you can to appear larger: raise your arms, open your jacket, throw stones or branches without crouching, wave your arms slowly, and speak firmly in a loud voice. query: nuclear detonation shelter in place guidance

This simple protective measure could save hundreds of thousands of lives in a major city. Everyone within 50 miles of the detonation should shelter in place. Go to the basement or middle of the building, staying away from outer walls, windows, and the roof because radioactive material will collect there. Close windows and doors, and if possible turn off units that bring in air from the outside. Stay inside for 24 hours unless local authorities provide other instructions, as it is important to wait for radiation to decay. The best shelter location is in the center of a large building of heavy construction (concrete, reinforced brick, cement) or in basements and underground areas such as parking garages and subways, which provide the highest dose reduction factors. If in a manufactured or mobile home and you cannot reach a brick or concrete structure, shelter in the middle of the structure. If you were outside after fallout arrived, remove your outer layer of clothing, brush off any fallout, and if possible wash exposed skin and hair. query: venomous spider identification and envenomation treatment
ground_truth: The two primary venomous spiders in the United States are the black widow and the brown recluse. The black widow spider is identified by a red hourglass marking on its abdomen (or a pattern of red coloration on the underside); it builds webs between objects and bites have two puncture marks. Its venom is a neurotoxin that produces pain at the bite area spreading to the chest, abdomen, or entire body, with possible abdominal cramps, nausea, vomiting, rash, weakness, tremors, and sweating. Anaphylactic reactions can occur. Symptoms usually regress after several hours. An antivenin is available. The brown recluse spider (also called violin spider) is small, light brown, identified by a dark brown violin-shaped marking on its back, and has six equal-sized eyes (most spiders have eight). There is little or no pain initially, but within hours a painful red area with a mottled cyanotic center appears. The outstanding characteristic is an ulcer that does not heal but persists for weeks or months. The venom can cause severe skin necrosis requiring professional medical attention. For first aid: stay calm, identify the spider if safely possible, wash the bite with soap and water, and apply a cloth dampened with cold water or filled with ice to reduce swelling.
